validation.comparison_result: Wrong result: expected 1230113636.0101, actual 89.85. Difference: ~13.7 billion times smaller (catastrophic error).

Q6.notes: CRITICAL: Result is completely wrong. Got 89.85 instead of 1230113636.01. This indicates a fundamental defect in the aggregation logic, not a performance issue.

Q6.bottleneck: SEMANTIC: Broken atomic aggregation in parallel execution
Q6.root_cause: Lines 162-164 use broken CAS-based aggregation: `compare_exchange_weak(old_sum, old_sum + local_sum)` without proper loop semantics. The atomic variable `thread_local_sum[thread_id]` stores intermediate results per thread, but with work-stealing morsel execution, threads don't map 1:1 to thread_ids. Only the first successful CAS per thread_id wins; subsequent threads spinning on the same slot never accumulate. Additionally, the use of `compare_exchange_weak` without reloading `old_sum` on retry creates a live-lock where only one thread per thread_id succeeds. Result: only a fraction of work is counted in final_sum.

analysis.overall: CRITICAL FAILURE: Query produces completely incorrect results (89.85 vs 1230113636.01). This is not a performance bottleneck but a correctness bug in the parallel aggregation logic. The code assumes each thread processes predicatable morsels and accumulates into thread_local_sum[thread_id], but under work-stealing, this breaks down. The atomic CAS loop is malformed and only one thread per slot successfully updates. Root cause: broken parallel aggregation semantics — need proper thread-local accumulation followed by safe merge.

critical_fixes.issue: Catastrophic correctness failure: aggregation produces 89.85 instead of 1230113636.01 (13.7 billion times too small)
critical_fixes.fix: Replace broken atomic aggregation (lines 162-164) with proper thread-local accumulation pattern: each thread maintains local_sum in a register during its morsel processing. After all morsels are exhausted, accumulate thread-local sums into a separate vector[num_threads] without atomics, then sum all partial results. Avoid compare_exchange_weak entirely—it's not suitable for accumulating multiple updates from different threads into a single slot.
critical_fixes.description: The current code at lines 120-124 allocates thread_local_sum as atomic<double>[num_threads], but the CAS loop at lines 162-164 is broken. With work-stealing, multiple threads process morsels sequentially (via morsel_counter.fetch_add()), and each thread gets a different thread_id. However, when multiple threads try to atomically update the same thread_local_sum[i], only one succeeds per CAS attempt. The loop `while (!compare_exchange_weak(...))` should update old_sum automatically on failure, but the semantics are subtle and clearly not working here. SOLUTION: (1) Allocate partial_results[num_threads] as non-atomic doubles. (2) Each thread_id accumulates into partial_results[thread_id] without synchronization (threads are disjoint by id). (3) After all threads join, sum all partial_results into final_sum. This matches DuckDB/HyPer's thread-local aggregation pattern: local-then-merge, not atomic-then-merge.

summary: Q6 iteration 1 FAILS due to catastrophic correctness error, not performance. The parallel aggregation logic is fundamentally broken: atomic-based accumulation of per-thread sums fails under work-stealing morsel execution. The query produces 89.85 instead of the correct 1230113636.01. This must be fixed immediately before any performance optimization can be meaningful. Recommended fix: replace atomic compare_exchange_weak loop with simple thread-local accumulation (partition results by thread_id, no synchronization needed during scan, merge at end). After correctness is restored, profiling will reveal actual bottlenecks (likely I/O or SIMD filtering optimization).
